data.title: Palantir Scores Big As Pentagon Reportedly Adopts Maven AI Across All Military Branches
data.description:
Feinberg Orders Maven Embedded Across All Military Branches

Maven is a command-and-control platform that rapidly analyzes data from satellites, drones, radars, sensors, and intelligence reports, using AI to automatically flag potential threats and targets such as enemy vehicles, buildings, and weapons stockpiles.

Palantir and the Pentagon did not immediately respond to Benzinga‘s requests for comment.

A Windfall for Palantir’s Government Business

Designating Maven as a program of record streamlines its adoption across all arms of the military and secures stable, long-term funding — a significant win for Palantir, which already landed a U.S. Army contract worth up to $10 billion last summer.

The company’s stock has roughly doubled over the past year, pushing its market cap to nearly $360 billion.

Price Action: Shares of Palantir closed down over 3% at $150.68 on Friday. The stock was slightly up in trading after the bell, having lost about 10% so far this year, according to Benzinga Pro.

According to Benzinga Edge Stock Rankings, Palantir shares are showing an upward trend in the short and long-term, although the company’s Growth score ranks in the 98th percentile.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: Elon Musk Found Liable By Jury For Misleading Twitter Investors In $44 Billion Deal, Faces Potential $2.5 Billion Damages
data.description:
Jury Finds Musk Liable Over Twitter Bot Claims

Jurors found him liable for two statements, including his claim that the deal was "temporarily on hold" pending verification of bot data and his suggestion that fake accounts could exceed 20%, Reuters reported.

However, the jury also found that shareholders failed to prove a separate allegation that Musk orchestrated a scheme to defraud them.

Billions In Potential Damages At Stake

Damages have not yet been finalized, but plaintiffs' attorney Francis Bottini estimated losses could reach as high as $2.5 billion, the report said.

The lawsuit represents shareholders who said they sold stock at depressed prices between May and October 2022 due to Musk's public comments.

"Musk's status as the world's richest man is not a free pass," Bottini said. "If you’re able to move markets with ​your tweets you’re responsible for the harm you cause to investors."

Musk Plans To Appeal Verdict

Musk's legal team pushed back, arguing his concerns about bots were genuine and not fraudulent.

In a statement, his attorneys called the verdict "a bump in the road" and said they expect to prevail on appeal.

Twitter Deal And Ongoing Legal Battles

Musk ultimately completed the acquisition of Twitter in October 2022 and later rebranded it as X.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: 'It Finally Happened,' Says A Millennial Who 'Graduated Into An Old Person' After They Fell For A Phishing Email Despite Being Tech-Savvy
data.description:
A millennial who always considered themselves tech-savvy recently shared how they fell for a phishing email, calling it a moment that made them feel like they had finally “graduated into an old person.”

“It finally happened,” the person wrote in a recent Reddit post that quickly gained traction. “I have never fallen for a phishing scam, but today they got me.” They explained that they had been online since childhood and understood how scams work, which made the experience even more surprising.

Why This One Worked

What made the scam effective wasn't poor judgment, but how normal everything looked. The email came from a known contact and matched something that person would realistically send.

Don't Miss:

The message appeared to be an event invitation, something that fit perfectly with recent conversations they had been having with that contact.

The login page also looked legitimate, and the process felt familiar. 

Adding to that, the moment wasn't ideal. They were out, dealing with low reception, and assumed any login issues were just technical problems. That small distraction was enough to lower their guard.

They realized what had happened within about an hour after noticing strange email behavior, including undelivered messages. From there, the response was immediate.

“I spent all night changing passwords, checking recovery pathways and verifying my information hadn't been changed,” they said.

Fortunately, the damage was limited. The compromised password wasn't reused elsewhere, and no financial tools were tied to the account.

Still, the emotional impact stuck. “There's a certain part of me that feels fully graduated into an ‘old person’ now,” they added.

Trending: Skip the Regrets: The Essential Retirement Tips Experts Wish Everyone Knew Earlier.

Scams Are Getting Harder To Spot

The Reddit post resonated with people, many of whom shared similar experiences. A recurring theme was that scams have changed significantly in recent years.

Additionally, scams are no longer obvious attempts promising huge payouts. Instead, they now look like everyday messages, for example, job offers, delivery notices or simple invitations.

That change means the biggest risk isn't lack of knowledge, but timing and context. Several people described falling for scams when they were expecting a message, waiting on a package or dealing with something urgent.

“Creating that sense of urgency is how they get you,” one person noted.

Even workplace phishing tests are catching people off guard. Some said they had ignored obvious scams for years, only to click on something that perfectly matched their current situation, like a flight change or a compensation update.

The original poster seemed to come to the same conclusion. They reflected on how they used to help older family members with technology issues, only to realize they are now at a similar stage in life.

“Now I am the age my parents were when the internet was first entering households,” they wrote.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: A 'Ramsey Show' Caller Says She's Going To Prison For Bank Fraud She Didn't Commit. Her Husband Of 23 Years Used Her Name During COVID
data.description:
A woman says she's heading to prison for a crime she claims she didn't knowingly commit, and she says it all traces back to a 23-year marriage marked by financial control and abuse.

Speaking on “The Ramsey Show,” Ann from West Palm Beach, Florida, told hosts Dave Ramsey and Ken Coleman that she's scheduled to report to prison in early June after pleading guilty to conspiracy to commit bank fraud. The case, she said, stems from actions her husband took during the pandemic using her name.

A Marriage Defined By Control And Financial Abuse

“I've been in a financially abusive marriage for the last 23 years,” Ann said. “Any money was his money. I never had a say in anything we did with the money.”

Don't Miss:

According to Ann, her husband handled all financial matters, including bank accounts and business filings. During COVID, she said, he put her name on documents and listed her as the owner of companies without her full understanding of what was happening.

“My husband put my name down on documents showing that I was the owner of a couple of companies,” Ann said. “He gave me different people to put down on these companies.”

When federal agents later questioned her, Ann said she confirmed signatures that were hers, but maintained she didn't understand the broader scheme.

She ultimately pleaded guilty after her attorney advised she likely wouldn't win at trial.

Facing Prison While Trying To Rebuild

Ann said she expects to serve three years, though she hopes the time could be reduced. Her husband has already served time related to the case, completing about one year of a three-year sentence before being released.

Trending: Skip the Regrets: The Essential Retirement Tips Experts Wish Everyone Knew Earlier.

The couple has four children, ages 22, 18, 17 and 12. Despite the situation, Ann said her husband still maintains control over her day-to-day life.

“He takes my keys if I try to leave. He's let the air out of my tires,” she said.

When Ann said her husband “won’t give” her a divorce, Ramsey pushed back on the idea that her husband could block it.

“He doesn't get the option of giving you a divorce,” Ramsey said, urging her to file immediately. “You live in Florida. You can file for divorce and it will be granted.”

Ann said she plans to do exactly that once she begins her sentence.

“My plan is the second I set foot in that prison that I'm filing for divorce,” she said.

Hosts Urge Fresh Start After Prison

Beyond the legal situation, Ann said her biggest concern is what comes next. She recently worked in epidemiology at a clinic, but worries a felony conviction will limit her future.

“I don't believe that I am very marketable,” she said.

Coleman encouraged her to prepare for re-entry now by being honest with future employers and building a support system.

“You have to try with everything you have to get out the correct narrative and look people in the eye,” he said.

Ramsey framed the situation as a chance to reset her life after years of abuse.

When Ann shared that “the year he was away was the best year of my life,” Ramsey responded with,  “Welcome to the rest of your life.”

Situations like Ann's highlight how financial control can leave one partner disconnected from critical decisions — and vulnerable to serious consequences. Experts say rebuilding often starts with understanding your finances and getting objective guidance. Services like Money Pickle connect individuals with vetted financial advisors to help regain control and plan next steps.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Whoopi Goldberg, 70, Says She Can't Afford to Retire Yet — She's Got Bills To Pay. 'If You Don't Marry Well, You Gotta Keep Working'
data.description:
If retirement had a slogan, it would be simple: work, save, stop. Whoopi Goldberg gave that idea a reality check—and it didn't come out gently.

It's the kind of line that sounds familiar—right up until you stop and look at who's saying it.

Don't Miss:

She has been framing it this way before

Goldberg didn't suddenly land on this idea in one interview. She has said versions of it before, and just as plainly.

During a 2024 Hot Topic segment on "The View," she said, "I appreciate that people are having a hard time. Me, too. I work for a living." Then she added, "If I had all the money in the world, I would not be here, okay? So, I'm a working person, you know?"

She pointed to family as part of the reason. "My kid has to feed her family," Goldberg said. "My great-granddaughter has to be fed by her family. I know it's hard out there."

Those comments draw a straight line through how she talks about work. For her, stopping isn't just about hitting a number—it's tied to responsibility.

Marrying “Well” vs. Working Hard

Goldberg's advice on “marrying well” comes from a woman who has walked the aisle three times—and decided she's much better off in the driver's seat alone.

Trending: Think you're saving enough for your kids? You might be dangerously off — see why

For Goldberg, the “marry well” line isn’t just a quip; it's a reflection of her reality. Since she isn’t splitting the mortgage with a spouse, the weight of supporting four generations of her family falls squarely on her shoulders. It turns out, when you're the CEO of your own dynasty, there's no such thing as an early exit.

The income paying her "bills" 

Goldberg has spent nearly two decades on "The View," and that role still anchors her income.

According to Parade, she earns about $8 million a year. Her net worth is widely estimated at around $20 million as of this year, built across film, television, and producing.

That financial picture doesn't cancel out what she said. It does, however, change the scale. Her "can't afford to retire" sits in a very different lane than most people's version of the same sentence.

The gap that people immediately noticed

Here's where the numbers stop being background and start doing the talking.

For a typical retail or service worker, retirement often leans almost entirely on Social Security, which averages about $2,071 a month. That has to cover housing, food, healthcare, and everything else. Many keep working into their 70s because stopping simply doesn't cover the basics.

Financial guidelines suggest having 8 to 10 times a person's annual salary saved by age 60 to maintain a similar lifestyle. Most people aren't close.

Now put that next to Goldberg's situation. A reported $20 million net worth and an $8 million annual salary don't remove obligations—but they do create a safety net that most workers don't have.

That's why the comment didn't just pass as relatable. It hit a nerve.

Same words. Same idea about "having to work." Completely different math behind it—and that difference is what people are reacting to.

For most Americans, retirement planning is a much steeper climb. Services like Money Pickle help connect individuals with financial advisors who can guide them on saving strategies, budgeting, and building a retirement plan — offering support for those who need to bridge the gap between current savings and long-term goals.

Image: Shutterstock
